speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six of the interview with George Ginsburg on the 4th of September, 1996. George, they-- you were talking about how they finally accepted that you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the reason was that the American intelligence has previously recuperated prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Well, we won't go--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, now, that's very important. That's very important, I'm going to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of SS personnel, high officers, have killed their prisoners and taken the identity and tattoo-ated them on the left hand and took their names as well. So that is why the suspicion was, when they took me prisoner, that's why they didn't know who I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before identifying me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we want your personal testimony of things. That's a very important thing that happened, I understand, but we'll continue with what you saw and what happened to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened to me was very simple. I was, in that time, they treated me very well. They took me to their headquarters. I was found by the Third Army Blackhawk division, Panzer Blackhawk division, which was the armored-- armored division. It was in Bavaria. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't remember the village by heart. It was a village where we were situated. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sort of looking after him, helping him in interpretations, explaining a few things to the CIC Counterintelligence Corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they got you to do some work for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I started to identify SS who were tattoo-ated on the left arm, [? blood ?] [? group. ?] I knew all that. They didn't even know the ranks between the German-- they only knew the German Wehrmacht ranks, they didn't know the SS ranks at all. They were not prepared for it. I knew that. A lot of things happened in that particular time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he asked me if I wanted to act as an interpreter and a scout for a few-- you know. I said, certainly. After a while, he got-- in the meantime, we were sending telegrams and telexes to Brussels to identify me properly. They were not stupid.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in contact with Brussels and they were asking Brussels if there was a George Ginsburg registered in Brussels, so and so and so and so and so. There's appeared on transport for the-- they got all the information they wanted. All right? So that-- that was a-- official confirmation.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, they asked me if I want to-- if I'm interested of-- of giving out to the Allied flag to the Americans. Or me, being non-American, I could be attached [BACKROUND NOISE] at the Belgium to the United States Forces. So I said, yes. So I-- they arranged all the papers and they arranged it with Brussels. And there was a Captain Blanche-- white, means white-- who arranged it too, with me. And I was accepted in the United States Forces. I attached from Belgium, from the Belgium Forces, so-called. And I-- I was helping him as a guide. I was also preparing him scripts in German for the Germans to surrender. So I went in-- in a little town, in a tank house-- a tank house in-- through loudspeakers, in German, to announce that the troops should lie down their arms, that the war is finished, it's all gone, and so [? et cetera. ?] So I made-- I-- I-- I helped them to do all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wrote the scripts? Or you-- you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote the scripts.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wrote both. I wrote the script, and I was announcing in German. And then-- and then I was-- they-- they put me onto one unit, which is called [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] ICI, Intelligence Corps Information, and there were people of-- from all countries in the United States Forces. There were Italians. There were Germans. There were all nationalities combined, even Russians. And they were all in the intelligence, American intelligence. There were wearing no signs, they could move anywhere, they were a unit on its own. They were intelligence unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was helping them. I was with them. I was wearing, you know, full uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I do? [LAUGHS] I was helping arresting people. [LAUGHS] I was going with them on patrol. And I was bringing them to jail and registering them, like they did to me. There was a Burgermeister of one particular thing, there was a thing of another. You see, in that particular time was a Werwolf was going on, called Werwolf. Werwolf was an ex-Nazi organization who were fighting the Allies, already after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was to eliminate them, to help the Allies. So I was in that particular unit. I was in the-- I was going with Colonel Friedman. I couldn't-- I was once-- I was locked up for four days by the Americans. He had to. He didn't lock me up, but he put me on house arrest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was brutalized, brutal. You know, I let my temper go. I shouldn't have done that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you let your temper go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, some-- some officers. I treated them the same way then they treated me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sort of officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they American officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. SS officers. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: SS officers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Army-- army and SS officers. And-- and he explained, he sat me down and he said, the reason we are here, he said, is to bring democracy to the world. If we do the same the Nazis did to you, and we do the same to them, we are not much better. We are here to introduce democracy and freedom. We are not here to brutalize the Germans. We are here to punish them by legal means. Not-- not like you want to do. And that's what he explained it to me, and I understood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, after that, I was with him for quite a while. I was in Mannheim, I was here, I was there. And on my helmet, I had a specially-- the people said to me, are you Deutsch? [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Are you German, American, they ask me. I told him to-- where if he has to speak with me, he has to speak over there and I'm over here, you know, to keep his mouth shut when he speaks with me. They couldn't understand the way I talk to them the same way that they talk to me. And we were identifying a few, and they were arrested, they were taken out of the camps. Who did you identify?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mission was to go to prisoner camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And identify who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazi-- the SS. The war criminals, to get them out. Or to suspect them of being war criminals. Because a lot of them took on Wehrmacht uniform, completely as Wehrmacht, but they were SS. But you know who gave them away but their own people. The Wehrmacht guys said, he is not a Wehrmacht, he is SS. You look at it. He took those uniforms. They hated each other. The Germans themself didn't like the SS. But you see, there were differential between SS. There was the Waffen SS, who was a fighting SS, and then there was a political SS who never fought.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your job was to find the SS. It doesn't matter who they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Waffen SS were soldiers of the SS. They were not important.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were looking for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were supervising the Wehrmacht. You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: So who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: --if they turned around, they'd shoot them in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- but who were you looking for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The political SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: The political SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazi party SS. Those people. And then I went to-- I got, well, a few instances happened there which were not very methodical. On interviews, say for instance, I did little jokes like that. I put the swastika flag in front of the door and we were all sitting there, these are including the [INAUDIBLE] officers and then the prisoners were brought in here to trip on a swastika flag before he comes to the thing. Things like little things like that. [? Enjoy ?] their laughter. And I was also active in-- in-- in one of the takings of-- of four German parachuters in a-- in a village. We had to go in, I went in. I was weaponed completely, everything. And we were-- we found them. They found them. I-- I-- and I gave the farmer five minutes to leave his house. And Colonel Friedman told me, when we come in the next town-- [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] was the town. Find me a nice house, two story, with a garden and a bit of a fenced off security.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I drove with a jeep with two guys there. It was already occupied. I saw a nice house, I went in there, I knocked on the door, the two guys have their guns outside. And I tell the fellow and the woman, I give you 10 minutes, take your coffee pot, your blanket, and get out of here. That's taken by the-- And who--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's taken by the United States Forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: German farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, but you said something about parachuters.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that was on a farm. That was a different story. We had to take it with shooting, with fire. We had to fight for it to get them out. They were German parachuters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- what did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Trying to escape, and couldn't escape the plane, and they parachuted there. We want to know who it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what did you do with these--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they fought back, they shoot back, but we got them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You shot them. They were shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you shoot anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what happened after your period-- how long were you with the American army in this way?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940-- '46. End of '46. Occupation force. '46. And then I went to Belgium, I got a discharge. I met a young girl, 17 years of age, Jewish girl. Dutch. She was a survivor. In the meantime-- ah, yes, I forgot to tell you. In the meantime, I knew that my mother was alive. That's the most important part.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out she was alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the Jewish Brigade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when--

speaker: interviewee
text: In American forces. And the Jewish Brigade was doing searches for families through the Joint Distribution Committee. And the Jewish Brigade, who was in-- in Germany, finding people. And they told me, we found a Mrs. Ginsburg in Hannover in a DP camp. I couldn't believe it. Then I went there. I got a permit and a driver and a jeep, and I went to Hannover. I-- [LAUGHS] I found her. And I brought her back. She was there, and she had two suitcases with underwear and silk underwear and dressing gowns. I left it all there. And she was crying, fur coat. And I took her in the jeep. She survived Berlin. She was working as an interpreter for the United States Forces in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- where was your father? What had happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- my father was hiding till 1944. April, 1944. And then he went up to have a shower in the apartment, and they were waiting for him. My mother was in the cellar. She didn't even know they took him until she came out. And he was sent to Auschwitz. And that particular transport-- Jews-- there was no more Jews after '42. But they were hiding, there were few there were-- they found a few-- couple of thousand Jews in Berlin, hiding, itself. And then I took my mother and I took her to-- I wanted to take her to Belgium, to my uncle. I didn't know what to do. And one of the young girls from the Jewish Brigade gave her a uniform. And she stayed in Brussels, and she gave her uniform to me. And I took the uniform to Germany. And I put my mother, the uniform on. And I said, don't speak. She doesn't speak English, she only spoke Russian and German. Don't speak a word. And I put her on a train, in the Allied train. She was sitting in a uniform, captain's uniform in an Allied train. And I was in the other compartment. I always send messages, if-- if that lady is still-- the captain is still sitting. I said, you read a book, and anybody speaks to you, just just pretend to sleep. And I took her over the border, and I took her to the Belgium station, to Brussels. And we arrived in Brussels, I took her-- she walked next to me. I was in Yankee uniform. She was in a British uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: '46. End of '46, '46. And then there was military police there, controlling. And there was an officer sitting to allocate accommodation to the leave people-- people who are leave. And I had to pass the military police with her. She had no papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I did is I went there to the cops, and I started a commotion. He said, oh, he said-- the American-- the American entrance is over there. Not here, here's only for British personnel. And I told him, I started-- you know, I start to assault him. I started to sort of call him names. And he called me names, and he-- he call-- he blew the whistle and more coming. And then Americans saw there's a [? commotion-- ?] and they came running.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Americans' MP came running, because he saw me. And by that time, I pushed my mother through, she just walked through. In the whole commotion, she walked out, out of the station. I nearly got arrested, I couldn't care less. The Yanks took me and put me out there, and you should not talk to the Limeys. Limeys, they called them. The British, they were called Limeys. You don't know the-- Limeys. Anyhow, I went out and she went to my uncle, and I went to Brussels, and everything was well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from-- in Brussels, you were saying you met a young woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to the-- a-- an open air dance. I danced with a girl, jitterbugging and jiving and all that. And she did was a good dancer, slim, lovely. And then we discovered-- I discovered she's Jewish. I got taken in her home, she introduced me to her parents and things like that. Her-- her-- her parents were in Switzerland, Genoa. They came back. And I was taken like a son there, you know. And then-- and I was in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you get married?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to Australia. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. By [INAUDIBLE], a troopship came to Australia under [? Kohler, ?] God bless him. If that wouldn't be for [? Kohler, ?] there wouldn't be any Jews here. Arthur [? Kohler ?] was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in Australia when you first came?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in [? Carden. ?] Funny. I was very fascinated, new country and all that. You know, I never had a peace, I never had life, I never had youth. I was very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a rehabilitation [? code, ?] which the Americans paid for. I learned cut and design and ladies' fashion. I worked in Flinders [? Lane. ?] The Americans paid for the course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your wife work too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She worked at [? Myer's ?] as a [? manicure, ?] but she was already pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did you have together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked on a ship, by the way. On the ship coming here, we both worked. And I got 200 pounds for the passage, each. I was like a steward, and so was she.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you did with that money when you came to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got a-- I got a little house on [? Paradise ?] [? Street ?] and things like that. And, you know. And then I had one daughter, when I [? were ?] [? here. ?] And then I had another daughter. And I-- I worked. And then from there on, I started a shop in Footscray and the little factory. And I sold that, then I went on the road, selling. And I was a representer for a company. And then after, say, eight years, I think it was, I lived in Melbourne. I lived in Sydney, I took her parents out, things didn't go too well. Because I shouldn't have taken her family out. She was more attached to her family than she was to me. And things got-- and I used to travel a lot, being away a lot. I was young, and things fall apart a bit. And we got divorced.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you get divorced?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the '50-- '50-- '50-- '52, something like that. And-- '53. And then-- then I was single. I lived in Melbourne. I saw my children, took them to Bulla, there, there, from Sydney. They all lived in Sydney. And-- and then I got married again, eventually, after 10 years. I was 10 years single. I met the English girl. She was born in the blitz. And I had four children with her, two boys and two girls. And first of all, before I had the girls, I immigrated to Israel from here, in 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a fantastic job in Israel as a marketing manager, and they sent me overseas. They sent me to Germany, for [INAUDIBLE] and so on, doing business. I was representing kibbutz industry. And I was in the office, in a big office in Tel Aviv. It's a government operation. I was like a traveling salesman, international. And because of my languages and knowledge and things like that. And in Israel, in 1972, my children went to school there, my two boys, Greg and Mark. And in 1972, the Yom Kippur War started. I had two options, to go on the airplane, leave back for Australia, take my children out of school, and just run away. The other option I had is to sit home and watch television behind drawn blinds. That's not me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I volunteered for the home guard, first. So they gave me a bit of a [? borrowed ?] [? shirt, ?] the home guard, and-- and I was in Karmiel, north of Galilee. And then I found out that I was in the tank division and all that, and they asked me to come in. So I accepted dual nationality. But you can't get foreigners into Israeli army. You have to be Israeli. So they asked me if I accept their nationality. So I had dual nationality, accepted it, signed it, and they put me in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in-- in Julis in a tent camp. Ready to go to Sinai. And then my wife came up with the car, it was already second day, saw my baby. That's [? Avivah ?] who was born then. She was born just three months before the-- is born in Israel. And-- and I went into the Sinai, and I was in a tank, and I was in Fayid, and this, and that, and that. And I went through it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the reason I came back is because my wife's parents became sick in [? Bentley, ?] here. Her father got sick from the heart and she wanted to see her father. Then-- and the mother, I got leave [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the army. Because I worked for a company called [? Coor, ?] [? Hevrat ?] [? Coor, ?] that's an export company. And after the war, I lost my job there because they sacked the foreigners first before they sacked the Israelis. Because they were-- they were older, longer time in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to Australia for a visit. I still had a house in Ra'anana. That's where I was living, Ra'anana, [INAUDIBLE]. And I came here, her father passed away and her mother got sick. And Tali was born here. My wife was pregnant. So eventually, eventually, eventually one came to the other and I stayed here. Maybe it was a mistake, maybe it was good, who knows. And that's my story, more or less, is it? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I served in the Israeli forces as well. And what I was saying, what I wanted to go back to, that when I came to Australia, I was very active in an organization called Betar. And I was part of-- I'm proud to be part of the founders together with a man called [INAUDIBLE] [? Camille, ?] who was in Jewish Brigade. Who was here-- passed away here. [? Camille ?] family is still there. And a fellow called-- he's still alive, a heart specialist called [? Eric ?] [? Stock-- ?] Mr. [? Lamb, ?] and Joseph Steiner, who is in Israel. He's-- he was a founder of the Australian Betar. And I was very active. And I run camps for the youngsters and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you religious at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm traditionally religious. I believe in God, I respect religion. I know all about it because in Berlin, I was forced to go to a-- after they kicked me out of the Gymnasium, I was forced to go to a Jewish school. Which happened to be [? Adas ?] [? Israel. ?] Couldn't have been better. They were checking our sandwiches, if we had kosher sandwiches there, brought from home. So I know all about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is there anything that you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been back to Berlin last year, to my hometown. I saw my apartment still standing there, beautiful. And I-- I looked at the places I-- as a boy, as a young man. I-- we went on tour to see a lot of Jewish places, you know, hospitals, synagogues, shuls, museums. It's all there now, re-established. The shul I used to go to and things like that. Some of them are placards, some of them are there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction to all of that, going back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very emotional. Very. There were 160 of us from all over the world, Berliners. We were invited from the mayor of Berlin, from the [? Senate, ?] which is the-- which is a townhouse, they paid for everything for seven days. Including my flight. Tali was there, too, and I took my boy with me, one of my boys with me as well. So it was very emotional, it was-- all came back to me. Everything. Even the streets. I looked, after 10 minutes I knew exactly where I was. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that your experiences during the Holocaust have affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It has, in a way, because A, I was never thinking about tomorrow. Never came to me in my mind. I-- I laughed at Australian people who-- who-- who planned when they retired, and tomorrow, and in 15 years, and in 20 years. I couldn't grasp it. Because you see, I was living from day to day. With me, it's from day to day. And that's my mind, was brainwashed, it was from-- oh, maybe, most of it is from month to month. And that's-- that's it. That's why I didn't accumulate any properties and things like that, you know. For whom, for what, you know? And it-- it-- it probably affects my children as well, you know, in those respects. I'm getting a pension from Germany but, you know. What I went through. And the operation I had now is also part of it, part of my punishment. On my back, when you get older, you get arthritis around the cracks. And that's why I had the operation on the spine. It was straining my-- my [? core ?] spine. They had to operate and remove two bones from it. So it did affect me quite a bit in the beginning. In the beginning when I was here, I was waking up in sweat and dreams and nightmares. I have got nothing of that anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about how you feel about other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I feel about other people? Well, a lot of them I cannot understand, because the sufferings of other people seems to me so minim. So little. With what people suffered during the war. And there's a lot of very no good people around, people which are really no good. And sometimes I'm thinking, isn't it pity that so many good ones really had to go. Thousands, hundreds of thousands. And you know, I think within myself that people, unless they haven't suffered, it doesn't leave a mark on them. Only people-- not only through the Holocaust, but even people who went through the war. Even if you take an Australian non-Jew who went through the war, he's a different type of man than a fellow never been through anything. Much more mature, broader outlook, more understanding of human needs and things like that. Cares more for people than other people do, that what my opinion is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel any bitterness towards anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, I do. Particular, particular to some of those Ukrainians and Poles, very bad people. And the-- in my opinion, they even haven't changed. You've got a lot of good ones. But, you know, they suffer too. But those simple people, those people who have made life terrible, the kapos and the [? unterkapos ?] of those nationalities. And even after the war, what I heard from other people in Poland was terrible things happen. Naturally, I feel bitterness to any-- any German who's about my age. Any German, any. And I think they were all the same. And I think that right there in the book, it's quite right to say that the German people are just as responsible as the SS. Not only the SS. But today, it's a different generation. Three generations ago. You never forget. You never forgive those people. But the people today is bending over to make peace with us and with Israel, and we should be tolerant towards this generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pleasure, pleasure. Right.
